context: TypeScript codebase. ReservationManager is a known shallow module the team wants grilled directly. It has methods: createReservation(customerId, restaurantId, partySize, date, time, smoking, vip, depositCents, depositRequired, notes) and updateReservation(id, partialFields). Three callers across api/reservations.ts, jobs/reminders.ts, and admin/reservations.ts each duplicate input validation (party size > 0, date in the future, deposit >= 0). The interface leaks Stripe payment intent IDs into the create signature even though only one caller uses deposits.
expected_behavior: Skips Phase 1 (--no-explore is set) and goes directly to Phase 3 grilling on ReservationManager
Phase 3 opens by re-stating the deletion test for this module: 'If ReservationManager were deleted, where would the complexity go?' — and resolves it before designing a new interface
Co-designs an interface, not handed down. Asks clarifying questions about invariants, ordering, error behavior, and configuration before proposing types
Classifies the dependency category (in-process / local-substitutable / remote-owned / true-external) — Stripe is true-external, restaurant DB is local-substitutable
Proposes promoting the scattered party-size / date / deposit invariants into the module (locality win) and hiding Stripe payment intent IDs behind a Deposit type (leverage win)
Proposes a testing plan tied to the new interface — what becomes integration-testable, what becomes unit-testable, what becomes contract-testable against the Stripe adapter
Names a seam (the deposit-collection step) and explicitly notes whether one adapter or two are expected — invokes the 'two adapters means a real seam' rule
Asks before generating an ADR. Only writes an ADR if the user gives a reason that should prevent future re-suggestion
Still does not refactor code or write the new module — output is the co-designed interface + dependency strategy + testing plan
failure_modes: Skips the deletion test and goes straight to interface design
Designs the interface unilaterally instead of co-designing — never asks for invariants or error behavior
Adds a port (interface + adapter) for a single implementation, violating 'two adapters means a real seam'
Writes the actual TypeScript implementation of ReservationManager v2
Auto-generates an ADR without asking — pollutes docs/adrs/ for a deepening that may still happen
